narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a strong family income background, your law school application was carefully evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of first-time success on the bar exam based on academic and background factors from past students. The model estimated your probability of failing the bar exam at 48.7%, which tipped the scales toward rejection because the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to pass on their first try. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school grade decile, which ranked quite low at 1 compared to the typical student's 5.5—suggesting a dip in performance that the model sees as a strong warning sign for bar success. This outweighed your solid LSAT score of 39, which is above the average of 36.7 and actively worked in your favor by signaling good test-taking ability. Your high family income also helped pull the prediction toward passing, as it aligns with resources that often support strong outcomes. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 was slightly below the average 3.21 but still contributed positively, showing decent prior academic foundation. Meanwhile, your lower Year 1 decile of 3 (versus the usual 5.7) nudged things toward failure, though less forcefully than Year 3. Enrolling full-time, like most students at 92.7%, provided a small positive boost. Compared to typical applicants, your LSAT and family background stand out as strengths, but the late-grade decline sets you apart in a concerning way that dominated the model's view. While this rejection stings, it's not the end—consider retaking courses or boosting recent grades to strengthen your profile for other schools. You might also target programs with bar passage support tailored to students like you. Reach out to admissions counselors for feedback, and keep pursuing your legal ambitions; many overcome similar hurdles. We're rooting for your next steps.
